verdict: Your ideal client — a B2B service business owner who's sick of looking like everyone else — will read your homepage and feel found within five seconds, which is unusually rare in this category. Where you lose them is the trust check at the moment of decision: anonymous testimonials, case studies with no numbers, and no external reviews to back any of it up — a careful buyer doing one last bit of homework on you walks away.
scope_note: Quick note before you dig in — this audit looks at your strategy, messaging, and content. It doesn't grade the design. Design matters, but it needs context about your business, your clients, and what you're trying to pull off — and that's a conversation best had on a call rather than in a written report. Everything below is about whether your site is doing the sales job it could be doing, not what it looks like.

fixes.title: Your testimonials don't say who said them — so they barely count as proof
fixes.detail: The two quotes on your homepage say things any prospect would love to hear, but there's no name, no business, no photo, and no date attached to either. To a buyer who's careful with their money, that reads as 'this could be made up' — and the goodwill the rest of your site has built quietly drains away.
fixes.action: Pick your three happiest clients, ask each for a one-line quote with their full name, business name, and a headshot, and put them on the homepage with the photo right next to the quote.

fixes.title: Your case studies tell the story but skip the numbers — and numbers are what close
fixes.detail: Duke's Plumbing case study has a great quote about people no longer haggling, but no actual figures — no leads-per-month, no revenue change, no traffic data. The buyer reading it wants to picture themselves in those numbers, and without them, the story is just a feeling.
fixes.action: Go back to two or three of your best clients and ask for one specific metric each — enquiries up, revenue up, ad spend down — and put it as a big number at the top of every case study.

fixes.title: There's no path for someone who's interested but not ready to book
fixes.detail: Every button on your site sends visitors to the same place — book a call. A serious buyer doing research isn't ready for that on visit one, so they bookmark the site and very often never come back. You're missing the warm middle ground.
fixes.action: Add one low-commitment option — a downloadable checklist, a 'questions to ask any web designer' guide, or an email signup tied to your insights — so visitors can engage without picking up the phone.

fixes.title: Your Insights section last published in 2021 — it's actively making you look inactive
fixes.detail: All five blog posts are from 2020 and 2021. A prospect doing due diligence on you opens the Insights tab, sees the dates, and quietly downgrades you in their head — even though the rest of the site says you're sharp and current. A dead blog is worse than no blog.
fixes.action: Either commit to publishing one new piece per quarter starting now, or remove the Insights link from the nav and lean into the FAQ as your content asset instead.

fixes.title: There are no reviews anywhere a buyer would actually look for them
fixes.detail: We searched for you on Google reviews, Trustpilot and ProductReview.com.au and found nothing. Word-of-mouth referrals will absolutely Google you before they enquire — and the silence at that moment costs you the ones who play it safe.
fixes.action: Set up your Google Business Profile this week, then send the five clients you're closest with a one-line message asking for a review with the direct link.

findings.check: One clear next step on every key section
findings.note: Your 'Lets Talk' button repeats consistently and is the only call to action — visitors are never confused about what to do next.

findings.check: Getting in touch is genuinely easy
findings.note: Your contact page is a form behind a Cloudflare check, with no booking calendar — and you explain why, which is fair, but it adds friction for the warm prospects who'd happily self-book.
findings.rec: Keep the form, but add a Calendly link below it for prospects who already know they want to book — your note about sales spam can apply to the form only.

findings.check: 3 clicks or fewer from landing to enquiring
findings.note: Homepage CTA → contact form → submit. Two clicks. Clean.

findings.check: Main call to action AND a lower-commitment option both present
findings.note: The only path forward anywhere on the site is to book a call — there's nothing for the visitor who's interested but not ready to talk yet, and that group is the majority.
findings.rec: Add one downloadable resource or email signup — a checklist, a pricing guide, anything — so the not-yet-ready visitor has somewhere to go.

findings.check: It's clear what happens after someone gets in touch
findings.note: Your contact page says 'I'll be in touch to book in a time to chat' — better than nothing, but no timeline, no idea what the first call covers, no sense of what to expect.
findings.rec: Add two lines — 'I reply within one working day' and 'the first call is 30 minutes, no pitch' — directly above the form.

findings.check: Navigation is set up for first-time visitors, not existing clients
findings.note: Home, About, Portfolio, Insights, Lets Talk — clean, conventional, no client login or industry jargon getting in the way.

findings.check: The page builds a case from start to finish
findings.note: Homepage moves from problem (everyone looks the same) → process (how we do things) → proof (case studies + quotes) → action — a tight, intentional flow.

findings.check: Clear differentiator
findings.note: Your stance — bold voice, anti-AI, 20 years of business experience instead of just design chops — is specific and not something a competitor could lift wholesale.

findings.check: Specialist, not generalist
findings.note: You're clearly positioned for B2B service businesses tired of being a commodity, and you turn away clients who want to dictate the design — that's a specialist signal.

findings.check: Offer clarity
findings.note: A visitor can see what you do, how the process works, and roughly what it costs — all without having to enquire. That's rare in this category.

findings.check: Why this business over the alternatives
findings.note: You hint at it (the FAQ on 'we know exactly what we want' addresses the cheap pixel-pusher option), but you don't directly tackle why a buyer should pick you over a bigger agency or a freelancer with similar pricing.
findings.rec: Add one paragraph or comparison block explaining why you specifically — not a generic agency, not a $2k freelancer, not a Webflow specialist.

findings.check: Honest about challenges and limitations
findings.note: Your 'Code' page and FAQs openly state who you don't work with — people who want to dictate design, AI-build fans, businesses with heaps of decision-makers — and that filtering builds real trust.

findings.check: Reflects current business
findings.note: Stale content surfaced under Pre-sold (recent insights). One home, no double-counting.

findings.check: The site opens in the client's world, not the business's
findings.note: 'When everyone's website looks like a ChatGPT prompt, trying to sound professional won't fill your pipeline' — that's the visitor's frustration on the page within seconds.

findings.check: The core problem the client faces is named clearly
findings.note: Looking like every other competitor, getting beaten down on price, sounding generic — these are the actual pains your ideal client carries, and they're called out plainly.

findings.check: Opening headline connects to the client's desire or situation
findings.note: 'Websites that stand out and get leads' lands the desired outcome immediately — the right buyer knows the page is for them.

findings.check: The emotional frustration behind the practical problem is addressed
findings.note: You name the practical pain (more leads, looking the same) but skip the deeper feeling underneath — the founder pride that suffers when your site doesn't reflect how good the business actually is.
findings.rec: Add one line in the opening that names the frustration of being proud of your business but embarrassed by the website you point people to.

findings.check: The bigger picture — what's really at stake — comes through
findings.note: You touch on premium rates and ideal clients being 'desperate to work with you', but the consequences of staying on the current site — slow decline, wasted ad spend, watching competitors win — aren't fleshed out.
findings.rec: Add a short section on what continuing to look generic costs over the next twelve months.

findings.check: The site shows it understands the client's situation before it sells anything
findings.note: Both the homepage and About page open with the visitor's situation before mentioning anything you do.

findings.check: What life looks like after working with this business is described
findings.note: 'Leads coming in like clockwork' and 'competitors crying' are great phrases but vague — the visitor doesn't get a concrete picture of what their week looks like once the site's working.
findings.rec: Add a paragraph painting a specific 'after' picture — fewer 'how much do you charge?' enquiries, more inbound from the right people, room to raise rates.

findings.check: There's a reason to act now, not later
findings.note: There's nothing pulling the visitor to act today rather than next quarter — no scarcity, no cost-of-waiting, no calendar constraint. The 'small number of projects' line is buried in a Notion-style closing block.
findings.rec: Surface the project capacity constraint near the main call to action — 'I take on three projects a quarter and the next opening is X'.

findings.check: Clear within 5 seconds what you do and who for
findings.note: Hero, sub-headline, button — a visitor knows you build websites for service businesses that want to stand out, in roughly two seconds.

findings.check: Headings pull the reader forward, not just label sections
findings.note: 'Win Clients not awards', 'Frequently (UN)Asked Questions', 'Get Ready to Stand out' — these earn the read rather than just labelling sections.

findings.check: Copy is written about the client, not the business
findings.note: The hero and FAQs do a good job of speaking to the visitor, but the About page and process section drift heavily into 'we' — the client disappears for long stretches.
findings.rec: Rewrite the About page so every other paragraph addresses the visitor directly — 'you've spent years building this' rather than 'we have 20 years of experience'.

findings.check: Services describe what the client gets, not just what the service is called
findings.note: Your toolkit on the About page lists 'Brand Positioning — How you'll win in a crowded market' which is closer to outcome — but most lines are still tool-flavoured rather than 'here's what changes for you'.
findings.rec: Rewrite each toolkit line so the outcome leads — 'Stop competing on price' rather than 'Brand Positioning'.

findings.check: No empty claims that any competitor could also make
findings.note: Your voice is too specific to slip into 'passionate, dedicated, tailored' territory — even your throwaway lines have personality.

findings.check: Long copy is genuinely scannable
findings.note: Short paragraphs, bold pull-outs, clear sub-headings — a busy founder can skim the page on a phone and still get the message.

findings.check: Testimonials are specific, named, and show a real result
findings.note: Both quotes on your homepage are anonymous — no name, no business, no photo, no date. Without attribution they're indistinguishable from copy you wrote yourself.
findings.rec: Replace both with named, photographed quotes from real clients — names and businesses are non-negotiable, headshots are the difference between 'maybe real' and 'definitely real'.

findings.check: At least one case study with a real, measurable outcome
findings.note: Your case studies have client names and a quote, but no measurable outcome — Duke's says people stopped haggling, but there's no number behind it. A buyer can't picture themselves in 'felt better about pricing'.
findings.rec: Add one specific number to each case study — enquiry count, revenue lift, conversion rate, ad spend reduced.

findings.check: Logo strips and badges show genuine proof
findings.note: Your NSW Government Approved Supplier badge is genuine external credibility — but you have at least seven case study clients and not a single one of their logos appears on the homepage.
findings.rec: Add a client logo strip on the homepage with the eight or so businesses you've actually worked with.

findings.check: The About page makes the owner human AND connects to why they do this work
findings.note: Nick's story and his 20 years of experience come through clearly, and the 'why' is genuinely tied to the client problem — but there's no real photo of Nick visible, just placeholder graphics where the headshot should be.
findings.rec: Get a real photo of you — laptop on lap, not corporate headshot — onto the About page so visitors can put a face to the voice.

findings.check: Qualifications and credentials are visible
findings.note: Beyond the NSW supplier badge, there are no third-party credentials shown — no platform partnerships, certifications, or affiliations a buyer could independently verify.
findings.rec: If you have any meaningful platform partnerships (WP Engine, Cloudflare partner, anything similar), surface them — and consider applying for one if not.

findings.check: Proof sits near the point where a decision is made
findings.note: The two testimonials are in the middle of the homepage, but the contact page itself has zero social proof — at the exact moment a visitor is about to enquire, there's nothing reassuring them.
findings.rec: Add one named testimonial with a photo directly above the contact form.

findings.check: Reviews and independent proof from outside the site
findings.note: Searches for you on Google reviews, Trustpilot and ProductReview.com.au returned nothing. There's no third-party signal a careful buyer can find when they Google you before enquiring.
findings.rec: Set up your Google Business Profile this week and ask five clients for a review — that single move closes the biggest credibility gap on this audit.

findings.check: Pricing is addressed openly
findings.note: $5–8k landing pages, $12–18k full sites, $25k+ for complex builds — clearly stated in the FAQ. This is rare and a major credibility signal in your industry.

findings.check: A visitor can work out whether this is right for them without contacting
findings.note: Between the FAQ and the 'Code' section, a visitor can self-qualify or self-disqualify before they ever pick up the phone — exactly the right behaviour to encourage.

findings.check: Content exists — real, recent articles a visitor can actually learn from
findings.note: Your blog has five posts, all of which appear to date from 2020–2021 — one post even references new year goals for 2021. To a prospect doing diligence, the Insights section reads as abandoned.
findings.rec: Either publish one new piece per quarter starting now, or remove the Insights link entirely and lean into the FAQs as your content asset.

findings.check: Content covers the topics buyers actually search for
findings.note: The FAQ covers the heaviest buying questions (cost, time, platform, AI), and the blog touches conversion rate and SEO — but there's no comparison content (Webflow vs WordPress, agency vs freelancer) which is what buyers actively search.
findings.rec: Write one comparison piece — 'how to choose a web designer' or 'WordPress vs Webflow for B2B' — that addresses the question your buyers are already asking Google.

findings.check: FAQs answer the real questions buyers have before contacting
findings.note: Your FAQ is one of the strongest parts of the site — it answers price, timeline, AI, custom design, content writing, and platform choice in your own voice. This does heavy pre-sell work.

findings.check: Video content present
findings.note: There's video embedded in the homepage and About hero sections — that's a positive — but there's no founder-intro video and no client story video, so the visitor never gets to hear or see Nick.
findings.rec: Record a 60-second piece-to-camera intro from Nick — phone-quality is fine — and put it on the About page.

findings.check: There's a path for a visitor to get ready before they make contact
findings.note: The blog and FAQs exist, but there's no curated 'before our first call' sequence — a visitor has to dig around to self-prepare rather than being guided.
findings.rec: Add a simple 'before we talk' page linked from the contact form thank-you screen — three articles or one short guide that primes them for the call.

positives: Pricing is published on the homepage FAQ — that single decision builds more trust than most agency sites manage in their entire copy.
The voice is unmistakably yours — no other agency could publish this homepage, which is exactly what you tell clients they should aim for.
Your FAQ section answers the real, hard questions buyers actually have before they enquire — cost, timeline, platform, AI, custom requests — and it does it without flinching.
You're openly honest about who you don't work with, which is rare and quietly does a lot of qualifying work for you.
The NSW Government Approved Supplier badge gives external credibility no competitor can fake easily — keep it visible.
